data.title: Empire State Manufacturing Survey: Activity Edged Higher In February
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Manufacturing activity edged higher in New York State, according to the Empire State Manufacturing February survey. The diffusion index for General Business Conditions climbed 18.3 points to 5.7. The latest reading was better than the forecast of -1.9.

Here is the opening paragraph from the report.

Business activity edged higher in New York State in February, according to firms responding to the Empire State Manufacturing Survey. The headline general business conditions index climbed eighteen points to 5.7. New orders and shipments grew moderately. Delivery times were slightly longer, and supply availability was slightly lower. Inventories continued to expand modestly. Employment levels moved lower. Input prices increased at the fastest pace in nearly two years, and selling price increases also ticked up noticeably. Though firms expect conditions to improve over the next six months, optimism about the outlook dropped significantly.

Background on the Empire State Manufacturing Survey

The Empire State Manufacturing Index rates the relative level of general business conditions in New York state. A level above 0.0 indicates improving conditions, and below indicates worsening conditions. The reading is compiled from a survey of about 200 manufacturers in New York state.

Below is a chart of the current conditions and its 3-month moving average, which helps clarify the trend for this extremely volatile indicator. The current 3-month moving average stands at -1.6.

Since this survey only goes back to July of 2001, we only have two complete business cycles with which to evaluate its usefulness as an indicator for the broader economy. Following the great recession, the index has slipped into contraction multiple times, as the general trend slowed.

We saw a gradual decline in 2015 that rose back up in 2016, with a giant dip in 2020 due to COVID-19. The index quickly picked up again in 2021, declined for 2022, and gradually rose in 2023. The index kicked off 2024 with a sharp decline but rose during the back half of the year, reaching positive territory for the first time in over 2.5 years.

Here is an overlay of the current and future conditions (a six-month outlook).

OPTIMISM FALLSFirms expect conditions to improve in the months ahead, but optimism declined noticeably. The index for future business activity fell fifteen points to 22.2. Capital spending plans remained soft. Supply availability is expected to contract somewhat over the next six months.

Meanwhile, here's another look at the latest ISM Manufacturing Business Activity Index.

Let's compare all five regional manufacturing indicators. Here is a three-month moving average overlay of each since 2004 (for those with data).

Here is the same chart including the average of the five. Readers will notice the range in expansion and contraction among all regions.

ETFs associated with industrials and manufacturing include: First Trust Industrials/Producer Durables AlphaDEX Fund (FXR), Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLI), Vanguard Industrials ETF (VIS), and iShares U.S. Industrials ETF (IYJ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Reserve Bank Of Australia Highlights Inflation And Trade Risk In Hawkish February Cut
data.description:
The Reserve Bank of Australia cut its cash rate by 25 bps for the first time in more than four years, bringing the rate down to 4.10%. The move is the beginning of a new easing cycle that has been enabled by growing confidence that inflation is becoming less of a threat. In addition to that, the RBA has assessed that the slowing Australian economy needs some relief from the interest rate's current restrictive levels.

The rate cut was in line with market expectations, as analysts expected that recent inflation data would allow for the RBA to join the other major developed market central banks in their cutting cycles. The shift toward a more dovish position had progressed over the last few months and was accompanied by a downtrend in short-term two-year Australian bond yield after they peaked in mid-November 2024 at 4.18%. Since then, yields have fallen around -32 bps through the first day of the RBA February meeting. However, the RBA statements following the cut caused the two-year yield to bounce after the market sensed the easing came with a hawkish positioning.

Inflation Outlook

The RBA’s key message in its statement is that it has gained enough confidence on the path of inflation to start cutting its cash rate to a less restrictive level. It specifically referred to the recent slowdown in underlying inflation observed in the December CPI reading as the data point to focus on, saying that it “suggests inflationary pressures are easing a little more quickly than expected.”

The RBA is referring to the trimmed mean inflation reading of 3.2% YoY in the December 2024 release, which was a deceleration from the 3.6% YoY reading in September 2024 and the lowest since December 2021 but still above the pre-pandemic average. The headline CPI index also saw a substantial cooling from 2.8% YoY in September to 2.4% YoY in December (lowest since March 2021). This puts the CPI inflation rate squarely in the 2-3% target that the RBA seeks to achieve, with the trend in underlying inflation suggesting that could also reach the top end of the target range in the next two quarters.

However, it's worth noting that December 2024 CPI rates were heavily affected by government relief programs. The Commonwealth Energy Bill Relief Fund (EBRF) rebates had a significant impact on electricity prices, driving them down -9.9% QoQ and -25.2% YoY, compared to what would have been a 0.2% QoQ increase and only -1.6% YoY decrease without the relief. Similarly, the 10% increase in Commonwealth Rent Assistance (CRA) rates helped maintain rent increases at just 0.6% QoQ and 6.4% YoY, which would have otherwise risen to 7.8% YoY without the assistance program. These effects will definitely play into the RBA’s decision-making, as they will eventually cause inflation to pop back up when relief expires. So in the end, the inflation outlook is not 100% clear, though the short-term directionality of data is favorable to cuts.

Upside Risks

This uncertainty has forced the RBA to commit to a clear rate cutting path and, instead, puts policymakers in a data-dependent mode. The RBA notes that “upside risks remain” to the inflation outlook, highlighting two reasons to not commit to cuts:

  • The RBA points out that the labor market remains strong and “somewhat tighter than previously thought” which could put upward pressures on inflation through strong wage growth. The Australian unemployment rate ticked up slightly, from 3.9% in November to 4.0% in December, but the trend still points to historic lows in joblessness. The RBA has expected the unemployment rate to normalize toward pre-pandemic readings around 6%, but that hasn’t materialized. Due to that, the RBA suggests it may be underestimating the strength in hiring demand. If that's the case, the current pace of wage growth, which is deemed as “not consistent with sustaining inflation at the mid-point of the target band” could remain elevated for longer than the RBA would like to see.
  • The RBA also indicated that it's less sure about inflation staying in the target range in the long term, as its forecast for underlying inflation was “revised up a little over 2026.” This may be evidence of a shift in the RBA’s view of the neutral interest rate in light of persistently tight labor markets and a more resilient economy. In its February Statement on Monetary Policy, the key quote is “The anticipated recovery of GDP growth and lingering tightness in labour market conditions are expected to sustain some upward pressure on inflation." Additionally, it likely captures the uncertainty around new trade tensions between the US and its trading partners as a result of Trump’s potential move towards broad tariffs.

Monetary Policy Outlook

With these risks in mind, the RBA opted to not commit to a pre-determined rate cut path. The final section in the monetary policy release contains the rhetoric that characterizes today’s move as a hawkish cut. The RBA reasserts that “sustainably returning inflation to target within a reasonable timeframe remains the Board’s highest priority” with a clear emphasis on “sustainability.” It insists that monetary policy “has been restrictive and will remain so” after the February cut, as there are “risks on both sides.” The RBA does not want the market to misclassify today’s admission that progress has been made on inflation as a signal that sentiment has shifted completely (like the Fed did in September). Instead, it holds to a cautious outlook, with no indication of how long the cutting cycle will last.

Reactions to the meeting today suggest that the RBA was a bit more hawkish than expected. Capital Economics' Abhijit Surya says that the tone suggests easing will be gradual and that Capital Economics only expects two more cuts in the next few meetings. Damien McColough of Westpac sensed an even more hawkish RBA, suggesting that there are even doubts about what the central bank could do in the next meeting. At the same time, there were some hints of dovishness in the statement, suggesting the Bank may have to act in response to a negative growth impulse that could arise from trade tensions. The broader market traded yields higher in its reaction to the cut, as the 2-year and 10-year Australian government debt yields increased around 5 bps each to 3.90% and 4.49%, respectively.

Australian Equity Outlook

The nature of the cut could pose a threat to Australian equities in the near term, as the risks highlighted by the RBA are unfavorable. This could be seen in trading today, where the S&P/ASX 200 Index fell -0.66% and the S&P/ASX MidCap 50 Index fell -0.76%. Both sit near all-time highs, which have been partly achieved by the outlook that interest rates would be on a path lower in 2025 to relieve the restrictive credit conditions that have been in place since inflation spiked in 2022. That sentiment could quickly sour if there's a sense that either the number of rate cuts in 2025 will be lower than expected due to higher inflation or the number of rate cuts in 2025 will be higher than expected due to weaker growth borne out of disruptive trade policy in the US and its trading partners. The RBA has expressed both of these concerns in the February 2025 materials.

Therefore, I believe that Australian ETFs like the abrdn Australia Equity Fund (IAF) and its peers EWA and FLAU will see some downward pressure at the beginning of the year from macro headwinds even if the RBA’s cash rate drifts lower. Here are two key risks to keep an eye on:

  • With Australian stocks rising to all-time highs, valuations become a major concern. An expensive P/E ratio makes Australian equities less of a candidate for an international alternative to US equities, as one of the primary factors in that selection is how expensive a specific international market is compared to the US. IAF has a P/E ratio of 21.2x which makes it more expensive than other large international markets like the UK (17.7x), Canada (19.5x), Japan (15.7x), and others. Looking at a historical perspective, Australian equity valuations are 2 standard deviations or more higher than the 10-year average.
  • IAF and other Australian funds tend to be heavily weighted in financials, a sector that could suffer from a higher-for-longer scenario and weaker growth prospects. IAF’s portfolio specifically has a 33.5% exposure to the sector, the largest weight, with its largest holding being Commonwealth Bank of Australia at 11.1%. That stock was down -1.44% and other major Australian banks like Westpac (-2.97%) and NAB (-2.49%) were down more. Margin pressure in this sector will be a key headwind for Australian ETFs unless the RBA backs off of some of its hawkish language today.

With these risks in focus, I rate IAF as a Sell in order to avoid the near-term uncertainty in interest rates and elevated valuations. I await a better entry point in Australian equities which are trending toward all-time highs and would look to cheaper developed markets for an international alternative to balance exposure to US equities.

data.title: Weekly Treasury Simulation, February 14, 2024: Most Likely Range For 10-Year Yield Is 2% To 3% In 2035
data.description:
Summary

As explained in Prof. Robert Jarrow’s book cited below, forward rates contain a risk premium above and beyond the market’s expectations for the 3-month forward rate. We document the size of that risk premium in this graph, which shows the zero-coupon yield curve implied by current Treasury prices compared with the annualized compounded yield on 3-month Treasury bills that market participants would expect based on the daily movement of government bond yields in 14 countries since 1962. The risk premium, the reward for a long-term investment, is large and widens over the full 30-year maturity range. The graph also shows a decline at a steady pace for the full 30 years. We explain the details below.

For more on this topic, see the analysis of government bond yields in 14 countries through January 31, 2025 given in the appendix.

Inverted Yields, Negative Rates, and U.S. Treasury Probabilities 10 Years Forward

The negative 2-year/10-year Treasury spread ended on August 26 after 537 trading days. The spread is currently at positive 0.21%. The table below shows that the August 26, 2024 streak of inverted yield curves is the longest in the U.S. Treasury market since the 2-year Treasury yield was first reported on June 1, 1976. The second longest streak is 423 trading days starting on August 18, 1978.

In this week’s forecast, the focus is on three elements of interest rate behavior: the future probability of the recession-predicting inverted yield curve, the probability of negative rates, and the probability distribution of U.S. Treasury yields over the next decade.

We start from the closing U.S. Treasury yield curve published daily by the U.S. Department of the Treasury. Using a maximum smoothness forward rate approach, Friday’s implied forward rate curve shows the shortest maturity 1-month forward rates at 4.37%, versus 4.37% last week. After the initial peak, there is a decline followed by a gradual rise until rates peak again at 5.38%, compared to 5.37% the previous week. Forward rates stabilize at 4.04% at the 30-year horizon, versus 4.05% last week.

Using the methodology outlined in the appendix, we simulate 100,000 future paths for the U.S. Treasury yield curve out to twenty years. The next three sections summarize our conclusions from that simulation.

Inverted Treasury Yields: Positive Spread Now, 26.3% Probability by February 13, 2026

Many economists have concluded that a downward sloping U.S. Treasury yield curve is an important indicator of future recessions. A recent example is this paper by Alex Domash and Lawrence H. Summers. We measure the probability that the 10-year par coupon Treasury yield is lower than the 2-year par coupon Treasury for every scenario in each of the first 40 quarterly periods in the simulation.[1] The next graph shows that the probability of an inverted yield now peaks at 26.3%, compared to 28.9% one week before, in the 91-day period ending February 13, 2026.

Negative Treasury Bill Yields: 12.2% Probability by October 19, 2046

The next graph describes the probability of negative 3-month Treasury bill rates for all but the first 3 months of the next 2 decades. The probability of negative rates starts near zero and then peaks at 12.2%, compared to 12.1% one week earlier, in the 91-day period ending October 19, 2046.

Calculating the Default Risk from Interest Rate Maturity Mismatches

In light of the interest-rate-risk-driven failure of Silicon Valley Bank on March 10, 2023, we have added a table that applies equally well to banks, institutional investors, and individual investor mismatches from buying long-term Treasury bonds with borrowed short-term funds. We assume that the sole asset is a 10-year Treasury bond purchased at time zero at par value of $100. We analyze default risk for four different initial market value of equity to market value of asset ratios: 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%. For the banking example, we assume that the only class of liabilities is deposits that can be withdrawn at par at any time. In the institutional and retail investor case, we assume that the liability is essentially a borrowing on margin/repurchase agreement with the possibility of margin calls. For all investors, the amount of liabilities (95, 90, 85 or 80) represents a “strike price” on a put option held by the liability holders. Failure occurs via a margin call, bank run, or regulatory takeover (in the banking case) when the value of assets falls below the value of liabilities.

The graph below shows the cumulative 10-year probabilities of failure for each of the 4 possible capital ratios when the asset’s maturity is 10 years. For the 5 percent case, that default probability is 45.48%, compared to 45.53% from last week.

This default probability analysis is updated weekly based on the U.S. Treasury yield simulation described in the next section. The calculation process is the same for any portfolio of assets with credit risk included.

U.S. Treasury Probabilities 10 Years Forward

In this section, the focus turns to the decade ahead. This week’s simulation shows that the most likely range for the 3-month U.S. Treasury bill yield in ten years is from 0% to 1%, unchanged from last week. There is a 22.86% probability that the 3-month yield falls in this range, a change from 23.11% one week before. For the 10-year Treasury yield, the most likely range is from 2% to 3%, also unchanged from last week. The probability of being in this range is 22.21%, compared to 22.32% one week prior.

In a recent post on Seeking Alpha, we pointed out that a forecast of “heads” or “tails” in a coin flip leaves out critical information. What a sophisticated bettor needs to know is that, on average for a fair coin, the probability of heads is 50%. A forecast that the next coin flip will be “heads” is literally worth nothing to investors because the outcome is purely random.

The same is true for interest rates.

In this section we present the detailed probability distribution for both the 3-month Treasury bill rate and the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield 10 years forward using semi-annual time steps.[2] We present the probability of where rates will be at each time step in one percent “rate buckets.” The forecast for 3-month Treasury yields is shown in this graph:

3-Month U.S. Treasury Yield Data:

SAS3monthUST20250214.xlsx

The probability that the 3-month Treasury bill yield will be between 1% and 2% in 2 years is shown in column 4: 20.17%. The probability that the 3-month Treasury bill yield will be negative (as it has been often in Europe and Japan) in 2 years is 1.00% plus 0.03% plus 0.00% = 1.03% (difference due to rounding). Cells shaded in blue represent positive probabilities of occurring, but the probability has been rounded to the nearest 0.01%. The shading scheme is defined as follows:

  • Dark blue: the probability is greater than 0% but less than 1%
  • Light blue: the probability is greater than or equal to 1% and less than 5%
  • Light yellow: the probability is greater than or equal to 5% and 10%
  • Medium yellow: the probability is greater than or equal to 10% and less than 20%
  • Orange: the probability is greater than or equal to 20% and less than 25%
  • Red: the probability is greater than 25%

The chart below shows the same probabilities for the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield derived as part of the same simulation.

10-Year US Treasury Yield Data:

SAS10yearUST20250214.xlsx

Modeling Correlated Foreign Exchange Rates and Government Yields

The SAS HJM++ methodology is based on five decades of research by Prof. Robert Jarrow. Using the no-arbitrage conditions of Heath, Jarrow and Morton [1992] and foreign exchange rate no-arbitrage conditions from Amin and Jarrow [1992], foreign exchange rates and government yields in major markets are simulated jointly. The simulation is based on daily movements in government yields and foreign exchange rates since January 2, 1962. The resulting distribution of the yen-U.S. dollar exchange rate one year forward is shown in this graph:

Similarly, the forward-looking correlation between 10-year zero-coupon Bund and U.S. Treasury yields is shown below:

For more information on HJM++ scenario generation and no-arbitrage Monte Carlo simulation, please contact the author.

Appendix: Treasury Simulation Methodology

The Treasury yield probabilities are derived using the same methodology that SAS Institute Inc. recommends to its KRIS® and Kamakura Risk Manager® clients. A moderately technical explanation is given later in the appendix, but we summarize it in plain English first.

Step 1: We take the closing U.S. Treasury yield curve as our starting point.

Step 2: We use the number of points on the yield curve that best explains historical yield curve shifts. Using daily government bond yield data from 14 countries from 1962 through January 31, 2025, we conclude that 12 “factors” drive almost all movements of government bond yields. The countries on which the analysis is based are Australia, Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, New Zealand. Russia, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Thailand, the United Kingdom, and the United States of America. No data from Russia is included after January, 2022. The graph below shows which points on the zero-coupon yield curve are incorporated as risk factors and the order in which they are added to the model:

Step 3: We measure the volatility of changes in those factors and how volatility has changed over the same period.

Step 4: Using those measured volatilities, we generate 100,000 random shocks at each time step and derive the resulting yield curve.

Step 5: We “validate” the model to make sure that the simulation EXACTLY prices the starting Treasury curve and that it fits history as well as possible. The methodology for doing this is described below.

Step 6: We take all 100,000 simulated yield curves and calculate the probabilities that yields fall in each of the 1% “buckets” displayed in the graph.

Do Treasury Yields Accurately Reflect Expected Future Inflation?

We showed in a recent post on Seeking Alpha that, on average, investors have almost always done better by buying long term bonds than by rolling over short term Treasury bills. That means that market participants have generally (but not always) been accurate in forecasting future inflation and adding a risk premium to that forecast.

The distribution above helps investors estimate the probability of success from going long.

Finally, as mentioned weekly in the Corporate Bond Investor Friday overview, the future expenses (both the amount and the timing) that all investors are trying to cover with their investments are an important part of investment strategy. The author follows his own advice: cover the short-term cash needs first and then step out to cover more distant cash needs as savings and investment returns accumulate.

Technical Details

Daily government bond yields from the 14 countries listed above form the base historical data for fitting the number of yield curve factors and their volatility. The U.S. historical data is provided by the U.S. Department of the Treasury. The use of the international bond data increases the number of observations to more than 108,000 and provides a more complete range of experience with both high rates and negative rates than a U.S. data set alone provides.

The modeling process was published in a very important paper by David Heath, Robert Jarrow and Andrew Morton in 1992:

For technically inclined readers, we recommend Prof. Jarrow’s book Modeling Fixed Income Securities and Interest Rate Options for those who want to know exactly how the “HJM” model construction works.

The number of factors, 12 for the 14-country model, has been stable since June 30, 2017. The volatilities for each factor are updated monthly and available to subscribers to the KRIS interest rate and macro-factor data service.

Footnotes:

[1] After the first 20 years in the simulation, the 10-year Treasury cannot be derived from the initial 30 years of Treasury yields.

[2] The full simulation uses 91-day time steps for 30 years forward. This note summarizes just the first 10 years of the full simulation.

data.title: Halozyme Therapeutics, Inc. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Halozyme Therapeutics, Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: PayPal: A Turnaround Story With Promising Returns
data.description:
PayPal's Downfall And Signs Of A Recovery

PayPal (NASDAQ:PYPL), known to millions of users around the world for its payment processing system, announced its FY 2024 earnings on February 4th, 2025. The company was expected to report revenue of $31.71 billion, an estimate which was slightly beaten by $89.08 million, together with a minuscule non-GAAP EPS outperformance of $4.65 vs. $4.59. Investors didn't appreciate the soft earnings surprise. The uncertainties around Braintree, combined with the soft outlook, caused a significant sell-off of the stock.

The environment around the company has changed. Those investors on the pandemic-driven PayPal hype train have mostly gotten off, and the current shareholder base patiently waits for the ongoing turnaround to materialize fully. Unfortunately, the Q4 earnings didn't provide enough optimism to lift the stock price. Quite the opposite, an 11.4% drop rather indicates a disappointment. However, there are several positive developments that will likely drive growth in the long term. The new CEO, Alex Chriss, continues to redirect the company and make it something much bigger than just a payment processor while highly focusing on growth.

The environment around PayPal wasn't favorable for investors with a long time horizon for quite a while. The sudden spike in the popularity of the company's services ignited hype around the stock during the pandemic, driving the market cap to unsustainable levels. The then management seemed to be carried by the excitement of the market and may have assumed that the growth would continue for many years. Unfortunately for the investors, on February 1st, 2022, when the company announced its earnings for FY 2021, it became clear that the ambitious goals wouldn't be met. Since then, until February 8th, 2024, the stock price dropped a whopping 215.22%.

After more than a year with Alex Chriss behind the wheel, things look brighter. E-commerce, innovation, small bets on projects with significant upside potential, and numerous new partnerships seem to pave the way for PayPal. The stability brought by Alex Chriss in terms of strategy and clear goals shows the company in a different light and suggests that the best is yet to come.

PayPal's Monetization Driver

FY 2024 was a transition year for the company. Yet, if the ambitious plan comes to fruition, FY 2025 will be a year of progress. Alex Chriss was very specific about the initiatives with the highest priority for the next four quarters.

In '25, our key strategic initiatives will be to win checkout, scale Omni, grow Venmo, and accelerate SMB.

~ Alex Chriss

Among these key areas of focus that will likely be crucial to the successful transformation, Venmo has a special place in the company's game plan. It's not only one of the main drivers of the strategy to shift from a pure payment processing provider to a commerce company but also the segment with the most monetization possibilities. As the CEO emphasized:

Our task is twofold: first, continue to improve the social P2P payment experience that made Venmo a verb, increasing engagement and bringing on more users; second, drive adoption of our monetized products, including the Venmo Debit Card and Pay with Venmo.

These words carry a piece of information that seems to be overlooked by the market participants but it's incredibly important. Namely, Venmo became a verb. This means that there is a moat built around the service. It also suggests widespread adoption and indicates that the brand achieved certain awareness and became embedded in everyday language. "Google it" or "Venmo me" are expressions adopted by millions of people, which also means a scale reached by the product or the service. The management clearly understands the power of its P2P payment service and keeps running initiatives to popularize the brand among customers and merchants, which was emphasized by the CEO during the Q3 earnings call.

Our new leadership team is taking a fresh look at Venmo, and we're completely transforming and upgrading the user experience. We know that we inherited one of the strongest P2P brands and see an opportunity to prioritize innovations that unlock Venmo's value. We believe that Venmo will eventually have multiple monetization levers.

~ Alex Chriss

There are several ways to monetize Venmo, including partnerships and promotions with payback offers, encouraging people to use Venmo debit cards who would be able to spend a Venmo balance, incorporating in-app advertisements, or expanding Pay with Venmo, which would require retailers to buy the option to offer the service. In FY 2024, PayPal took advantage of some of the monetization opportunities and was able to drive higher revenues per active Venmo account, with the growth of Venmo Debit Card and Pay with Venmo monthly active accounts in Q4 by 30% and 20%, respectively. It is worth noting that it's still an early phase in Venmo's financial contribution to the company's top and bottom line.

Beyond that, introducing Venmo abroad would be a significant step forward and an additional growth factor. When it comes to Europe, the market of P2P payment services is very fragmented without a clear leader. PayPal has a massive advantage over the competition since it's broadly known to people and considered safe. A PayPal-branded P2P app could be a success story. And with the customer base dominated by Millenials and Gen Zers, the chances are solid that a young audience abroad would try it and likely stick to it. A firm statement that Venmo is getting positioned to become a revenue stream for PayPal came from the company itself not a long time ago.

We believe that we are only scratching the surface of Venmo's potential and we'll continue to focus on customer-back innovation to expand its reach.

~ IR Newsletter September 18, 2024

PayPal Is Reshaping

Over recent years, several segments of the economy have received ample attention from investors. There have been cryptocurrencies, electric vehicles, and AI-related booms and busts. What doesn't get that much coverage anymore is e-commerce, which remains an area of significant upside and innovation. It's expected to grow from $4.4 trillion in 2023 to $6.8 trillion in 2028 globally. While e-commerce is growing, digital payment processors will benefit from it, including PayPal, with its brands.

A good example of increased consumer spending is Black Friday, which once began as a one-day shopping event, transforming over time into Cyber 5, lasting five days, over which billions of dollars are transferred online. For PayPal, Cyber Monday was the most successful day of 2024 as of December 16, with around $7 billion in payments processed. Apart from achieving such a milestone, PayPal enabled numerous small businesses to process over 70 million transactions, helping more than 1.2 million companies achieve their best sales day of the year. Additionally, significant growth of Buy Now Pay Later (BNPL) and Pay with Venmo was observed, which recorded double-digit increases compared to Cyber 5 from the previous year.

Such a growth rate aligns with one of the key vectors driving the company's transformation: velocity. Before the new CEO stepped in, PayPal was moving too slowly. Although there were innovations such as expanding into cryptocurrencies, introducing BNPL, Pay with Venmo, launching PayPal Zettle in the US, or developing PayPal into a super app, the pace wasn't fast enough to keep up with the market expectations and also to outpace the competitors. To change it, Alex Chriss prioritized reshaping the team and shifting the mindset. PayPal's brand awareness should transition from the button at the checkout and a secure transaction into a service working across commerce.

It's worth noting that the company possesses an outstanding advantage by having data from millions of consumers and merchants while being a middleman who can connect them. In the era where data is the new gold, being in such a position is beneficial, and the focus lies on how to take advantage of it. Having transactional data gives PayPal enormous power to create a personalized experience for its customers, including rewards or discounts directly from the merchant. Such an environment will benefit not only buyers but also sellers, who will save money on advertising across huge demographics, often with little success.

Alex Chris' vision of commerce is focused on personalized experiences. The company's position in this environment is promising for the business and for its shareholders. Given the amount of data to leverage and the direction the company is taking, it may become a success story of the likes of Meta (META). PayPal is already creating an ad network, building profiles, and exchanging information with customers to define their needs and structure PayPal's offerings accordingly.

The business has a competitive moat that isn't fully utilized. The payment and commerce ecosystems offer a broad range of growth opportunities. Unlike its rivals, PayPal worked out its unique approach by focusing on both consumers and merchants. Yet, the vast majority of PayPal's competitors concentrate either on buyers or solely on sellers. With Alex Chriss, who comes across as a person with a clear vision, a bigger game beyond secure digital payments is PayPal's superior goal. The message from the management gives a sense that the company is moving to a new level and is outpacing its competition, although there is no confirmation of it in financial numbers. Nevertheless, evolving and finding new monetization opportunities should be the right approach in an environment flooded with services trying to copy PayPal solutions and steal its market share.

The idea of a moat - something that gives a company a clear advantage over others and protects it against the constant onslaught of competition - is essential to long-term success. Without a moat, eventually, someone else will find a way to do what you’re doing cheaper or better.

~ Charlie Munger

PayPal's Fair Value

While the developments look promising, PayPal's numbers for FY 2024 didn't spark excitement among investors. With revenue of $31.8 billion, the company grew its top line by 7.0% YoY increasing its higher operating to 17.7% from 16.3% the year prior. However, net margin deteriorated to 13.0% from 14.3%. It resulted in EPS growth of 4.7% YoY, not an increase that led to elevated optimism among investors. Likely, the guidance for FY 2025 wasn't compelling for those who were expecting a major upside. Yet, with the numbers for FY 2024, the outlook from the company, and the main strategic goals named by the CEO, a valuation with a long-term horizon should be assessed.

The valuation model is based on the global transaction value and, accordingly, PayPal's Total Payment Volume (TPV), including its growth until 2027. There are several assumptions included in the calculations, such as:

  • PayPal's TPV will grow marginally faster than the global total transaction value, corresponding to the company's market share growth.
  • Total Take Rate (total revenue divided by TPV) has been declining, and considering a worst-case scenario, it'll remain depressed.
  • After the Net Margin dip in 2022, a recovery has been observed. Reaching the levels from 2020 will be difficult, but minuscule steady growth can be expected.
  • The company is committed to share buybacks, which, together with the stock-based compensation and the release of new shares, should amount to roughly $4.5 - $6.0 billion in net repurchases a year.
  • The P/E multiple is much lower than the 5-year average. However, it represents EPS growth expectations, which are substantially softer today. A conservative assumption is that the ratio will decline over the next few years.

These assumptions lead to a fair value of $80.60 per share as of February 17, 2025, which almost aligns with the current stock price. At first glance, it doesn't look compelling. However, with the estimated EPS growth and the expected fair value of $127.33 per share in 2027, the annual growth of the investment would amount to 17.76% CAGR over the next 3 years, and it will likely be a market-beating return. Considering lofty market gains in the last five years, chances are that the next won't be fruitful for those investing in broad stock indices today. Hence, the returns offered by PayPal seem to be attractive.

Another indirect strong argument in favor of PayPal is the authorization of a new $15 billion buyback program. Together with the $6.0 billion worth of shares repurchased in FY 2024, this is a message from management that the stock is undervalued. Over the last five years, the share count decreased by a solid 15.3% while its underlying value was returned to the shareholders.

In addition, PayPal's stock downside is limited after the extended sell-off from a few years ago and the muted performance since then. On the other hand, considering all the initiatives and the company's positioning, the upside seems substantial. Following Charlie Munger's words of wisdom, it’s far better to buy a wonderful company at a fair price than a fair company at a wonderful price, and PayPal is a company worth investing in.

PayPal's Concerns

The leader of the digital payment space has its risks. A month ago, multiple and unconventional cyberattacks were aimed at the platform. The company handled the situation and issued warnings to its users. Cyber threats are not the only danger for the business. A common bearish case includes doubts about how well PayPal will execute its promises and whether it can keep up with the competitors, including Apple Pay from Apple (AAPL) or Google Pay from Alphabet (GOOGL). These tech giants have the resources and know-how to create excellent, competitive products that may negatively affect PayPal's revenues. Besides that, PayPal has a track record of not delivering on its promises and painting a rosy picture of its future self, such as the creation of a super app the likes of WeChat from Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY), a serious push into cryptocurrencies, or being on the path of becoming a $1.0 trillion company in the foreseeable future.

From a financial standpoint, net margin and TPV decreased, and the number of active accounts stagnated. On a positive note, the margin is at a safe 13.0% mark. In case of a tumultuous year, a substantial drop in net income can be absorbed. TPV numbers have been dropping for years, and hopefully, there has been an end to it. Yet, investors are focusing more on the transaction margin, which measures transaction profitability after subtracting transaction-related costs. This metric grew by 7% YoY, which is a positive sign. What was also raising questions in recent quarters was the number of active accounts. Although PayPal focuses on the quality of an account defined by the engagement and the number of transactions per account, a growth of this metric is something the market expects. A 2.1% increase YoY isn't an exploding rise, but it's still a return to growth.

Although PayPal is not risk-free, the ongoing and potential issues seem manageable. Investing requires patience, and the business is currently going through changes that need time to materialize and show up in financial results. Overall, the risk/reward proposition should be compelling for value-oriented investors.

PayPal's Future

In one of his interviews, the company's CEO said that PayPal is not short of opportunities. This becomes visible to consumers and merchants who enjoy the firm's new services and platforms. The investor base also seems to understand better the growth these initiatives will drive.

Overall, strong arguments can be brought to defend a bullish stance on PayPal, which overweighs risks and uncertainties around the company. With plenty of initiatives, clear goals, solid strategy, financial stability, robust growth, leadership in the payments space, expansion into commerce, worldwide trust, and more, the company can be considered a great business and has all the resources to thrive in the fast-changing, tech-dominated world. Those who don't mind buying a fairly-valued business that, however, offers significant upside may place a long-term bet on PayPal.

data.title: Tri Pointe Homes: Things Get Tougher From Here
data.description:
Today, I am putting Tri Pointe Homes, Inc. (NYSE:TPH) in the spotlight for the first time since my last article on this mid cap home builder nearly two years ago. The housing sector is increasingly being challenged by the lack of housing affordability across the nation as well as mortgage rates hovering near seven percent.

I noted these headwinds in my recent analysis around Beazer Homes (BZH). In addition, Tri Pointe Homes just posted its most recent quarterly result. Therefore, it seems an opportune time to circle back on Tri Pointe Homes and see how the company is navigating a tough housing environment. An updated analysis follows below.

Tri Pointe Homes is headquartered in Nevada. The company is focused on the building and selling of single-family attached and detached homes. Tri Pointe operates under six brands including Maracay, TRI Pointe Homes and Quadrant Homes. The firm primarily develops communities in Arizona, California, the Carolinas, Maryland, Nevada, Texas, Washington and Northern Virginia. California represents nearly a third of overall home building volume while Texas accounts for nearly a quarter. Currently the stock trades around $36.00 and sports an approximate market capitalization of $3.4 billion.

Recent Results:

As mentioned previously, Tri Pointe Homes posted its Q4 numbers before the bell on Tuesday. The company delivered GAAP earnings per share of $1.37, eight pennies a share above the consensus. Homebuilding gross margin rose 40bps from the same period a year ago to 23.3%.

Revenues fell just under one percent on a year-over-year to $1.23 billion, $10 million better than expectations. Digging into the numbers, home deliveries fell four percent from the same period a year ago to 1,748. Average home sale prices rose two percent to $699,000. Active home selling communities fell eight percent to 147. In addition, new home orders came in 940, which is down from 1,078 in the same period a year ago. Quite concerningly, order backlog dropped 35% to 1,512 homes. Order backlog value fell 28%.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

The analyst community is currently mixed on Tri Pointe Homes current prospects. While no analyst firms have currently chimed in on the just posted quarterly results, since mid-October there have been three Buy and three Hold ratings reissued from analyst firms.

Approximately four percent of the outstanding float in the shares is currently held short. There were very few insider transactions in the stock in FY2024 as insiders sold just under $9 million worth of equity collectively during the year. There has been no insider activity in the stock so far in 2025.

The company made some significant progress firming up its balance sheet and delivering for shareholders in FY2024. Tri Pointe Homes bought back nearly four million of its own shares during the fiscal year and increased its book value per share by 14.5%. The average purchase price of its stock buyback during the year was approximately $37.00 a share. The company also announced a new $250 million stock purchase program that expired at year end 2025.

Tri Pointe also paid off $450 million in senior notes and ended the year according to management 'with the strongest balance sheet and liquidity in our history'. It ended the year with homebuilding debt-to-capital ratio of 21.6%. The company closed its fiscal year with total liquidity of $1.7 billion, including $970 million of cash and market securities and additional credit availability of nearly $700 million.

Conclusion:

Tri Pointe Homes made $4.83 (GAAP) a share in FY2024 on $4.4 billion of revenue. GAAP earnings per share rose 40% on a year-over-year basis on a 20% increase in revenues. The current analyst firm consensus has profits declining to $4.50 a share in FY2025 as sales fall slightly.

That leaves TPH trading at 7.5 times trailing earnings and 1.3 times revenues. Now that is a deep discount to the valuations currently around the S&P 500. However, home builder stocks almost always trade at a significant discount to the overall market due the cyclicality of the industry.

Looking at the stock's historical price to sales and price to book valuations, TPH is not trading close to 'bargain' territory. It is important to keep in mind that earnings are also getting boosted significantly by stock buybacks. In addition, the stock is largely flat over the past 12 months even as the home builder delivered stellar earnings and sales growth in FY2024. How will the equity hold up in 2025 as both earnings and revenues are projected to decline slightly?

Given the continued challenged housing sector environment and rising home inventory levels across the nation, the risk/reward profile is not attractive on Tri Pointe Homes currently as FY2025 looks like it will be more challenging than its recently completed fiscal year.

data.title: Berkshire Keeps Buying Occidental Petroleum, But I Prefer Enterprise Products Partners
data.description:
OXY and EPD stock: Why I like energy stocks in general

Readers familiar with my articles know that I'm in general bullish about energy stocks under current conditions. A key idea underlying my bullish view is that I think oil prices are too compressed. I see several catalysts that could drive oil prices higher in the near term. Reader interest in more details about these catalysts (such as the low level of the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve and low overall crude stocks in the U.S.) are invited to take a look at my recent article on CVX.

Against this backdrop, the goal of this article is to analyze two other major oil stocks, Occidental Petroleum (NYSE:OXY) and Enterprise Products Partners (NYSE:EPD) from an additional different perspective: Insider activities. You will see that the latest insider activities also support a bullish view on both stocks.

I’ve covered both stocks before. I last wrote on OXY stock on 12-17-2024. That article was titled "Occidental Petroleum: Why I Agree With Wall Street." In that article, I commented on the prevailing sentiment surrounding the stock and rated the stock as a Hold based on the following considerations:

I reiterate my Hold rating on Occidental Petroleum due to a mixed outlook. OXY's Q3 earnings reflected some uneven segment performance. The balance sheet was another area of concern, with increased debt and new share issuances. Positive catalysts include reasonable P/E, a healthy long-term EPS outlook, and its ongoing divestment of non-core assets to raise cash.

As for EPD, my most recent analysis was published on 1-9-2025. In that article, titled “Enterprise Products Partners: Loads Of Catalysts For Sale At 12x P/E,” I rated the stock as a strong buy. As the title already gave away, the key considerations behind the rating are the cheap P/E and also the growth drivers. More specifically, I argued that:

Enterprise Products Partners recently declared a dividend raise, the second raise in a year. These dividend increases reflect strong business fundamentals. I expect continued dividend and EPS growth due to many catalysts, such as potential policy tailwinds from a Republican administration and its ongoing expansion projects. Despite these catalysts, the current valuation, either measured by its P/E or yield, is too low in my view.

Since the publication of these articles, there have been a few new developments surrounding both OXY and EPD. First, there's a price/valuation divergence since my last set of articles as you can see from the following chart. EPD has indeed outperformed OXY in the past few months by a margin of more than 12%. To wit, EPD saw a total return of 9.32% in the past three months. In contrast, OXY experienced a slight negative return of -3.38%. Second, the rest of this article will elaborate on the latest insider activities surrounding both stocks as aforementioned. In the end, consideration of these new developments has promoted me to upgrade my rating on OXY to Buy and reiterated my Strong Buy rating on EPD.

OXY and EPD: Insider activities in focus

I opened this article by stating that the latest insider activities support a bullish view on both OXY and EPD primarily for two reasons. First, the latest insider activities are dominated by purchases, in contrast to the insider selling on in the broader market (see the next chart below). And second, the insider purchases were made by key insiders.

More specifically, the chart provided below shows the latest insider activities surrounding OXY. As seen, Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) made multiple purchases of OXY stock. As a 10%-plus shareholder, BRK is considered an insider. As of Feb.7, 2025, the discourse showed that a total of 15 transactions were executed, all of which were purchases. The total amount of OXY stock bought was more than $879 million. The latest purchase, also disclosed on Feb. 7, shows that BRK purchased 763,017 shares at a price of around $47 per share. The current price of OXY is about $48, very close to that of BRK’s latest order. Given the continued interests in OXY demonstrated by Berkshire’s recent purchases and Warren Buffett’s value-oriented approach, this signals to me that the current price is an attractive entry point to OXY.

The insider activities surrounding EPD stock show a very similar pattern as you can tell from the following chart. Again, here we see only purchases and no sales. Specifically, there were two transactions, both purchases, totaling $185,757 made by A.J. Teague, Co-Chief Executive Officer of EPD. The most recent transaction occurred on Aug. 27, 2024, where Teague purchased 4,180 shares at a price of around $29 per unit. EPD’s current price ($33 as of this writing) is noticeably above this price (by about 13%). However, as to be detailed next, thanks to its up-beating earnings outlook, the stock is still attractive valued despite the price rally.

EPD vs. OXY stock: Valuation comparison

The next chart displays the P/E ratios for OXY and EPD across a few different metrics. As seen, based on the earnings projected for this fiscal year, OXY's FWD P/E ratio is 14.14, while EPD's is significantly lower at 11.64. Looking ahead to the next few years, we can see that both companies’ implied FWD P/E ratios are expected to further decrease due to the growth in earnings. For example, OXY’s FY2 P/E is expected to shrink to ~12 and EPD’s to ~11 only. These multiples – especially EPD’s multiples - are very attractive in my view, both in absolute terms or relative terms amid an otherwise very expensive equity market.

EPD vs. OXY stock: Dividends and consistency

Besides EPD’s more attractive P/E ratio when compared to OXY, I also prefer its more generous dividends and more consistent earnings. As a reflection of these considerations, the next chart compares the dividend data of OXY and EPD. As seen, OXY's forward dividend yield is 1.83% currently, while EPD boasts a significantly higher yield of 6.40%. Having a larger portion of the potential return paid in cash dividends adds certainty and downside protection to the investment.

EPD’s earnings are also more consistent. As a reflection, EPD has a long history of dividend growth (26 consecutive years) while OXY’s dividends were more erratic. OXY had to drastically cut its dividend recently (and thus has a negative five-year CAGR of -22.46%) but has been resuming its payouts in the past ~three years or so (and thus shows a three-year CAGR of 180.20%). In contrast, EPD’s dividends growth were more consistent with a 3-year CAGR of 4.98% and a five-year CAGR of 3.54%.

Other risks and final thoughts

In terms of other upside risks (that is, in addition to the currently compressed oil prices), I anticipate both OXY and EPD to benefit from the potential of increased domestic exploration activities, which seem very likely to me given Trump’s running platform during his presidential campaign.

My article so far has an obvious tilt toward EPD. To make the comparison more balanced, OXY has plenty of attractiveness in its own right. For example, OXY is less leveraged, and you can tell from a variety of metrics presented in the next chart. To start, OXY has a book value per share of $28.13, more than double EPD's $13.08 despite OXY’s higher stock prices. As a result, Finally, OXY’s price to book ratio (P/B ratio) is much lower at 1.71 than EPD's 2.56. OXY's debt/free cash flow is significantly lower at 13.70 compared to EPD's 36.60. Also, the long-term debt to total capital is 41.70% for OXY and 50.53% for EPD. Finally, when the valuation is based on EV (enterprise value), OXY has a forward EV/EBIDTA ratio of 5.71 , compared to EPD’s 10.05.

To reiterate, I feel very positive overall about both OXY and EPD due to my view of oil prices and the potential of policy tailwinds ahead. For OXY, I feel the price divergence since my last writing has made it valuation much more attractive. Its current price is very close to the most recent insider purchase price. For these reasons, this reexamination has led me to upgrade my rating on OXY to BUY from my earlier rating. I feel even more bullish about EPD due to its lower P/E, high dividend yields, and also better earnings consistency, all leading to a reiteration of my Strong Buy rating.

data.title: First Hawaiian's Near-Term Prospects Have Improved Significantly (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
First Hawaiian's (NASDAQ:FHB) near-term prospects have improved significantly since my last update in the summer. While I have always liked this bank from a quality angle, the near-term outlook for earnings growth was soft back then, meaning a then-multiple of 13.5x consensus 2025 EPS didn't look particularly enticing.

Since then, the outlook for interest rate cuts has moderated, the yield curve has steepened, and funding costs have fallen – all of which are good for the bank's earnings outlook. That has also given the shares a nice boost, with their ~19% return roughly matching the wider regional banking space in that time.

While the stock has delivered around 16% in share price appreciation over the past six months (the rest has come from dividends), near-term earnings prospects have increased by almost as much. Accordingly, FHB is trading at a roughly similar P/E to last time out. While the bank won't make anybody rich in a hurry, FHB can appeal to a certain type of investor, and given earnings growth should kick on from here, I am upgrading my rating a notch.

A Much Improved Outlook

I last covered FHB after Q2 2024 results. Since then, the bank has seen its net interest income ('NII') return to sequential growth, with second-half NII of $315.4 million up around 3% compared to H1.

NII accounts for around 85% of First Hawaiian's revenue, so developments here are key to its earnings. In turn, NII is driven by changes to the net interest margin ('NIM') and by growth in interest-earning asset balances.

The outlook for the bank's NIM has improved recently. Last quarter, this came in at 3.03% – up around 8bps sequentially, 11bps from previous coverage and 22bps year-on-year. On the liability side, FHB has seen deposit costs fall due to the reduction in interest rates. At the same time, it has also been using maturities in its investment portfolio to pay-off some of its more expensive forms of funding. Deposit mix-shift has also abated, with the bank seeing non-interest-bearing balances stabilize at roughly 34% of the total.

On the asset side, FHB's blended yield has also ticked down due to lower rates, but by a lesser degree, widening its interest rate spread and NIM. Additionally, the bank conducted a restructuring of its securities portfolio last quarter, selling $290 million of securities yielding ~2% and reinvesting the proceeds in assets yielding ~5%. While the bank booked a ~$19 million post-tax loss on these sales, it will pick-up an additional ~$8 million in NII this year (or ~$6.5 million after tax), so the pay-back period isn't that long. This happened toward the end of last quarter, contributing just 1bp to NIM, but it will contribute around 4bps to the 2025 margin.

Last time out, I saw the dynamics of FHB's investment portfolio as being bullish for the bank in the long run. This accounts for around 30% of its total interest-earning assets, but it was only yielding ~2.13% at the end of last year, depressing its earnings power. Using ongoing maturities to pay-off relatively expensive forms of funding, reinvest in newer higher-yielding securities, and/or to fund loan growth would all be a tailwind to NIM and earnings.

Management is opting for the first and third options. The yield on FHB's loan book averaged 5.55% last quarter, largely due to the fact that ~30% of it consists of long-dated residential mortgages (these had a blended yield of just 3.90% in Q4). Because of the significant step-up in new loan yields in recent years, the outlook for growth in these loans is very subdued. However, FHB is seeing better trends in commercial lending, with that powering solid growth last quarter on a sequential basis.

While the yields on FHB's commercial loans ticked down last quarter, they were still yielding over 6% – well above the rate of its investment portfolio, which ended the period with a ~2.15% yield. Accordingly, earning-asset mix-shift will be a nice tailwind for NIM here. Management expects ~3%-5% loan growth this year, so it will have an opportunity to realize this.

Credit quality remains resilient, with net charge-offs, non-performing loans and delinquent loans still broadly in line with pre-COVID marks. Most of FHB's loan book is secured by real estate. On the commercial side, its average loan-to-value ratio is less than 60%, giving it plenty of space to absorb potential losses. This isn't the type of bank that should keep investors awake at night.

Shares Look Okay For Conservative Investors

FHB currently trades for ~$27.20, having gained around 16% in price appreciation since my last update. Revisions to earnings estimates have brought consensus FY2025 EPS up by a similar amount in that time, meaning the stock is only marginally more expensive in terms of P/E (~13.8x compared to ~13.5x). Relative to year-end underlying tangible book value of ~$16.50 per share (tangible stockholders' equity excluding the unrealized losses on FHB's investment securities), the multiple is around 1.65x.

That is roughly fair for this kind of bank. The valuation is relatively high, but the bank's underlying return on tangible equity is also comfortably above my ~9% hurdle rate, warranting a premium to tangible book value. As I mentioned above, FHB is also under-earning right now due to its securities portfolio, so its underlying profitability is higher than current metrics suggest.

Finally, this is a bank that can easily afford to pay out most of its earnings to shareholders. In addition to the cash cost of its dividend (~$130 million), FHB has a $100 million buyback program in place for 2025, bringing its overall prospective payout ratio above 90%. That implies a total yield of just under 7%.

I expect the payout ratio to land closer to 80% over the long run – roughly in line with the pre-2023 average - and this would bring the yield down to circa 6%. Assuming FHB delivers long-term organic growth in line with nominal GDP (roughly 3%-4%), these shares could just about clear my ~9% hurdle rate.

Conclusion

In my previous update, I said that NIM expansion was probably a long-term prospect for First Hawaiian. Developments since then have been positive, with the bank's near-term outlook improving significantly as a result. That has contributed to a ~13% upward revision to consensus FY2025 EPS, while FY2026 EPS is seen growing another 9% on top. These shares won't make anyone rich in a hurry, but for investors in the market for a no frills bank stock, FHB might now be worth a look.

data.title: Medtronic Earnings Review: Stuck In The Single Digits Again
data.description:
Investment Overview

Medtronic (NYSE:MDT), the Dublin, Ireland,-headquartered medical device giant, announced its fiscal year 2025 Q3 earnings earlier today. In trading so far today, Medtronic stock is down >7%, trading at ~$86 per share, implying a market cap valuation of $110bn. Let's look at some key figures.

Medtronic generated $8.3bn of revenues during the quarter - up 4.1% year-on-year - and achieved earnings per share, on an adjusted diluted basis, of $1.39, up 7%. Its cardiovascular division accounted for $3bn of revenues, up 5% year-on-year, neuroscience $2.46bn, up 5.2%, medical surgical $2.1bn, down 0.4%, and diabetes $694m, up 10.4%, on an organic basis. Net income was $1.79bn, up 3.4% year-on-year.

On a year-to-date basis, revenues were up 4.8% on an adjusted, organic basis, to $$24.7bn, with a $9.2bn contribution from cardiovascular, up 5.8%, $7.26bn from neuroscience, up 5.7%, $6.25 from medical surgical, up 0.4%, and $2bn from diabetes, up 11.3%. Year-to-date net income was $3.6bn, compared to $3bn in the prior year, and basic EPS $2.8, up from $2.27 in the prior year period.

For full-year 2025, Medtronic states that from a base of $32.14bn forecast revenues in Q4 2024, organic revenue growth would be 4.75% - 5%, with FX headwinds of $(325m) - $(275m), and "other" deduction of $(125m) implying revenues of $33.5bn - $33.6bn. From an EPS perspective, from a base of $5.2 in 2024, EPS will grow to $5.44 - $5.5, up ~5.2%.

Analysis - Wall Street Struggles With Stodgy Growth

In my last note on Medtronic, covering fiscal year 2025 Q2 revenues, which also underwhelmed the market, I shared recent quarterly earnings breakdowns and noted that Medtronic's quarterly top and bottom line growth was somewhat unimpressive on a 3-4 year basis.

In fairness, the $8.4bn of revenues the company generated in Q2 was up from $7.98bn in the prior year, and $7.6bn in the year before that, and net income of $1.68bn was up from $1.54bn in the prior year, and $1.4bn in the year before that, with net income also improving, and, as per the figures shared above, Q3 represents another year of growth.

This time, however, let's take a look at annual income statements:

In 2016, Medtronic generated $28.8bn revenues and operating income of $5.72bn - in fiscal year 2024, the company generated $32.36bn of revenues, and $6.4bn operating profit, both up respectively ~12%. Arguably, it's not hard to see why Medtronic is not setting pulses racing on Wall Street, or why its share price is down 24% on a five-year basis, and trading at roughly the same value as it did at year-end 2016. It's not growing fast enough to meet investor's expectations.

With that said, the company pays a quarterly dividend of $0.7 per share, for a yield of 3.25%, and its forward price to sales ("P/S") ratio based on the midpoint of 2025 guidance is ~3.3x, which is attractive, while forward price to earnings ("P/E") ratio comes to ~20x, which is adequate to impressive.

In my last analysis I also made the point that Medtronic markets and sells hundreds, if not thousands, of products that treat more than 70 different conditions, so explosive growth from new product launches - even if Medtronic shares that it has launched ~120 new products in the last 12 months - is simply not on the agenda.

As Medtronic's CEO put it on today's call with analysts:

We delivered another quarter of mid-single-digit revenue growth for the ninth quarter in a row.

This is "good but not great" performance territory - Wall Street loves a top-line growth story, even if a company is loss-making, which helps to explain why Medtronic's incremental gains are not being rewarded with a rising share price.

Signs That Medtronic Has "Break Out" Potential In Certain Key Areas

I don't want to paint too gloomy a picture - on the earnings call, the CEO Geoff Martha pointed to strong growth in Cardiac Ablation Solutions - up 22% year-on-year, and affirmed his belief that:

This will be a $1 billion business for us this fiscal year, and we have line of site to $2 billion as our Pulse Field Ablation ("PFA") portfolio expands into new accounts around the world.

Cardiovascvular care is another growth area, and overall, the CEO did a good job highlighting multiple products making a major difference to patient's lives.

For example, - Evolut in structural heart and aortic... "our five-year low-risk data will be presented as a late breaker at ACC next month," cardiac pacing therapies... "our micro-franchise continues to set the standard, delivering outstanding 24% growth in Q3," hypertension... "we had a pivotal development when CMS opened a national coverage analysis," the spine market... " a major competitor decided to get out of the spine business," neurostimulation... "on top of being the smallest and thinnest SCS device, Inceptiv instantly adjusts based on neural responses to keep the therapy at an optimal dose," Parkinson's... "Percept devices through a software upgrade can become personalized, fully closed-loop systems with real-time automatic therapy adjustments based on brain activity feedback," and so on.

For me, diabetes is arguably the division that has the greatest room to grow thanks to Medtronic's MiniMed 780G system, which has only two major competitors - Dexcom's (DXCM) G7 continuous glucose monitoring device (780G is also a "CGM"), and Abbott Laboratories (ABT) Freestyle Libre. Medtronic also has market leadership in the automated insulin pump markets. MiniMed could be a $5bn per annum product by 2030, in my view.

In summary, there's much to admire about Medtronic the company, but less to admire about the trajectory of its share price. If madness is doing the same thing over and over, and expecting a different result, then Medtronic's guidance for 2026, which the CEO suggested would likely be another year of "4.75% to 5% organic revenue growth and high-single-digit realized EPS growth in the back half of the year," it might be madness to expect any major share price gains any time soon.

Pandemic Pressures Stymied Medtronic Growth In 2021, Which Hasn't Recovered

The reality is that Medtronic may still be feeling its way out of the difficult period it suffered during the pandemic, which hurt revenues, which fell from $30.6bn in 2019, to $28.9bn in 2020, triggering a slide in Medtronic's share price from >$130, to ~$75 per share.

Although Medtronic grew in 2021 and 2022 it shrunk in terms of top line revenues in 2023, and its shares have been unable to claim any upside momentum, never seeming close to recapturing former highs. Four years on from the advent of COVID, Medtronic clearly cannot blame lock-down or the absence of BAU working conditions for its current business performance any longer, but we can speculate that things may have been different if it had not been for that difficult period.

Looking Ahead - Single-Digit Growth Goes Unrewarded, Revenue Misses Punished

Wall Street has ultimately dumped Medtronic stock today because the company missed analyst consensus revenue expectations for $8.33bn thanks to the decline in medical surgical sales, which management put down to inventory destocking issues.

Furthermore, Medtronic missed its own target for 4.75% overall growth, and this was an issue Wall Street did not forgive, as the Street chose to ignore signs of progress and the fact the destocking issue was a "one-off" that would unlikely be repeated in fiscal year 2026.

Again, this seems to sum up the relationship the company has with investors at the present time. The company either needs to be perfect or stumble across an explosive growth product in order to trigger some share price upside momentum, but neither of those scenarios are especially likely given the dynamics of the industry Medtronic operates within - fragmented, low margin, and prone to unpredicatbility.

In my last note, covering Q2 earnings, Medtronic had also failed to win over the doubters, with shares down 3% after they were shared. However I awarded the stock a "Buy" rating, concluding:

Medtronic seems to be heading in the right direction, however, with eight straight quarters of year-on-year growth. The pace of innovation does seem to be picking up also, which opens up the prospect of continued top-line growth — as management has promised.

The bottom line is the major concern. It has been too flat and/or unpredictable to stimulate share price growth. However, I find myself agreeing with management that with good execution, the company has the product pipeline to grow top and bottom lines faster than the market expects.

There is also cash available for M&A — while Medtronic can't add billions of revenues to its top line in 2026 organically, it could do with some strategic M&A, so I would look out for news on that front.

Make that nine straight quarters with an improving bottom line, a cash position of nearly $8bn, and long-term debt off $24.6bn, which by the standards of a company Medtronic's size, is manageable - rated A by Standard & Poor's, and A3 by Moody's, so few concerns on that front.

When my last note was published, Medtronic stock traded at $85, and today it trades at $86.5 - so the message is clear - investors won't get rich buying Medtronic stock, but they will receive a low risk >4% dividend, and if the company does produce a faultless quarter, the share price could deliver double-digit growth in 2025 / 2026. Just don't count on it.
